currentSnapshot.definition: A person guilty or capable of great wickedness; the antagonist of a story.
currentSnapshot.usageNote: Firmly established as a term for moral evil. The 'villain of the piece' is both a narrative role and a real-world accusation.
currentSnapshot.exampleUsage: The villain of the story turned out to be the trusted adviser.

historicalSnapshots.definition: A serf or feudal tenant bound to the land; a farm worker of low social status.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: From Medieval Latin 'villanus' (farm worker, from 'villa', country estate). The word described legal status, not moral character — a villain was simply a peasant tied to a lord's land.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: The villains of the manor owed three days' labour per week to the lord.

historicalSnapshots.definition: A low-born, uncouth, or boorish person; one who lacks the refinement of the nobility.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: As feudal structures calcified into cultural prejudice, the legal term 'villain' acquired contemptuous overtones — the peasant class was assumed to be morally inferior as well as socially subordinate.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: He spoke like a villain, rough and without manners.

historicalSnapshots.definition: A deliberate scoundrel; a person whose conduct is base and criminal.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The class origin was forgotten as the moral condemnation intensified. Shakespeare's villains — Iago, Richard III, Shylock — cemented the theatrical archetype of calculated evil.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: O villain, villain, smiling, damned villain! — Hamlet, I.v

summaryOfChange.shortSummary: From 'serf on a farm estate' to 'epitome of moral evil' — class prejudice crystallised into a permanent moral label.
summaryOfChange.longSummary: The journey of 'villain' is a masterclass in how social prejudice gets baked into language. In medieval law, a 'villanus' was simply a serf bound to a manor — a legal category, not a moral judgment. But as feudal society assumed the peasantry to be brutish, unlettered, and untrustworthy, the legal term acquired contemptuous overtones. By the time of Chaucer the word suggested boorishness; by Shakespeare it meant calculated wickedness. The farm worker had become the archetype of evil, carrying the stigma of a class that had long since ceased to exist legally.

keyDates.label: Domesday Book
keyDates.significance: The Domesday Book of 1086 recorded 'villani' as the largest class of unfree peasants in England — a purely legal and economic category.

keyDates.label: Chaucer's contemptuous use
keyDates.significance: Chaucer uses 'villain' to describe boorish or low-bred behaviour, marking the transition from legal status to class contempt.

keyDates.label: Shakespeare completes the moral transformation
keyDates.significance: Shakespeare's 'villain' is a conscious, calculating schemer — no trace of the serf remains. The theatrical archetype of evil is set.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary — villain, n.
sources.attribution: Paraphrase and excerpt. OED content © Oxford University Press. Used for educational commentary.
sources.excerpt: Originally: a feudal serf or villein; a person of low birth or social status. Later: a person guilty or capable of great wickedness.
sources.relevanceNote: The OED traces the full arc from feudal legal term to moral condemnation.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary — villain, n. (sense 3)
sources.attribution: Paraphrase. OED content © Oxford University Press.
sources.excerpt: A person guilty or capable of great wickedness; a person who acts from base motives.
sources.relevanceNote: The fully evolved modern sense — moral evil completely detached from class origin.

sources.title: Merriam-Webster — villain
sources.author: Editorial staff
sources.excerpt: a character in a story or play who opposes the hero; a deliberate scoundrel or criminal.
sources.relevanceNote: Captures the dual modern use: narrative archetype and real-world accusation.

sources.title: Domesday Book (1086)
sources.excerpt: Villani: unfree peasant tenants, the most numerous class. Owed labour services to the lord of the manor.
sources.relevanceNote: Shows the word in its original purely legal context — a census category, not a moral judgment.

relatedWorks.title: Hamlet
relatedWorks.creator: William Shakespeare
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: Contains the pivotal moment when Hamlet calls Claudius a 'villain' in the fully moral sense — the word as a synonym for murderous evil rather than low birth.

relatedWorks.title: Feudal Society
relatedWorks.creator: Marc Bloch
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: Bloch's landmark analysis of medieval social structures explains the 'villein' class in its proper context and how feudal class assumptions shaped language.

relatedPages.title: Pejoration
relatedPages.summary: How and why word meanings become more negative over time.

relatedPages.title: Register and Slang
relatedPages.summary: How social class and register shape the meaning of words.

relatedConcepts.label: villein (legal)
relatedConcepts.note: The medieval legal spelling 'villein' is still used in historical and legal contexts to distinguish the serf from the modern 'villain'.

relatedConcepts.label: knave
relatedConcepts.note: Also began as a neutral term for a boy or servant, pejorated to 'rogue/scoundrel'.

relatedConcepts.label: boor
relatedConcepts.note: From Dutch 'boer' (farmer), pejorated through class prejudice to 'unmannerly person'.

ambiguityNotes: In English legal history, 'villein' (the alternative spelling) still appears in property law discussions of feudal tenure.
In narrative contexts, 'villain' can be used affectionately or playfully ('he's such a villain').
The word's connection to 'villa' (country estate) is a reminder that the peasant was defined entirely by the landowner's property.

timelineEvents.title: Domesday Book: the villani
timelineEvents.summary: The Domesday Book recorded 'villani' as the largest class of unfree peasants — a legal category with no moral connotation.

timelineEvents.title: Chaucer: boorish connotations emerge
timelineEvents.summary: By Chaucer's era, 'villain' had acquired overtones of roughness and low breeding — class contempt entering the language.

timelineEvents.title: Shakespeare's moral villain
timelineEvents.summary: Shakespeare's plays — Hamlet, Othello, Richard III — established 'villain' as the archetype of calculated moral wickedness.

timelineEvents.title: The archetypal antagonist
timelineEvents.summary: By the 20th century, 'villain' is standard in both fiction (narrative role) and real-world usage. The serf is entirely forgotten.
